title: Workbook — Man and Sin
html:
Student Workbook

For use after class or in a group. Print this page or work in your notebook.

Remembering the Essentials

  1. What does it mean that the human being was created «in the image of God»? What three capacities make us his image?
  2. How does Genesis describe the unity of body and soul in the human being?
  3. What was the serpent's strategy in tempting Eve? How did he distort God's word?
  4. What is original sin, and how is it transmitted? Why is it a «state of broken communion» rather than just an act of disobedience?
  5. What is concupiscence and how is it related to original sin?
  6. What is the «Protoevangelium» and where is it found in the Bible? Why is it so significant?
  7. What does Saint Paul mean when he says «where sin increased, grace abounded all the more» (Rom 5:20)?

For Reflection and Writing

Answer each question honestly. There are no «correct» answers to these personal questions — only truthful ones.

1. Saint Augustine says: «I was made and I unmade myself.» In what areas of your life do you feel that God's image in you is wounded? Where do you notice the tension Saint Paul describes: «I do not do what I want, but I do the very thing I hate» (Rom 7:15)?

Be specific. Name the area, the pattern, the struggle. This is not to shame yourself but to bring it into the light where grace can reach it.

 

 

 

2. The serpent tempted Eve with a single question: «Did God really say...?» What «serpents» whisper to you today that God does not love you, that his commandments are oppressive, or that sin isn't really that serious?

It could be social media, peer pressure, an inner voice, a cultural narrative you've absorbed. Name it honestly. What distortion of God and his love is it selling you?

 

 

 

3. God's first question to fallen humanity was «Where are you?» If God asked you that question today, what would your honest answer be? Are you hiding? Running? Ashamed? Slowly turning back?

Write your answer as a short letter beginning: «Lord, I am...» It doesn't need to be long. It needs to be true.

 

 

 

 

4. Compare Genesis 3 with the Annunciation (Luke 1:26-38). In the garden, a woman says «no» and sin enters. In Nazareth, a woman says «yes» and salvation enters. The Church Fathers called Mary «the new Eve.» What does this connection say to you? Where in your own life are you being invited to say a Mary-yes where you've been living an Eve-no?

Think of something concrete: a relationship, a decision, a way of living. Where is God asking for your trust?

 

 

 

5. Tolkien described history as «the long defeat» — the sense that sin keeps winning, generation after generation. Where do you see the long defeat in your family, your community, or your own heart? But Tolkien also gave us eucatastrophe — the sudden joyous turn of grace when all seems lost. Have you ever experienced a eucatastrophe? Where?

Sometimes grace shows up where we least expect it. Sometimes the thing we thought would destroy us becomes the doorway. Write what you see.

 

 

 

6. The Protoevangelium (Genesis 3:15) promises that the serpent's head will be crushed. In what area of your life do you most urgently need Christ to crush the serpent's head — to break a pattern of sin, heal a wound, or restore a broken relationship?

Name it. Bring it to Christ in prayer this week. He promised victory — and he keeps his promises.

 

 

 

Practical Activities

A. Read Genesis 3:1-15 Aloud — Then Read Backwards

First, read Genesis 3:1-15 aloud slowly. Write down: what did God actually say? What did the serpent say? How did the serpent distort it? Where does Eve add to God's word (Gen 3:3)? Then read the passage backwards — starting at verse 15 and moving up. When you reach the Protoevangelium first, before the Fall, how does the passage feel different? The promise is there from the beginning. God's response to sin precedes the sin itself in his providence.

 

 

 

B. Write a Letter to God Beginning: «Lord, I hid myself because...»

Write honestly. Not to condemn yourself, but to recognize that he is already seeking you, just as he sought Adam and Eve in the garden. He already knows. The letter is for you — to stop hiding, at least on paper. End the letter with a request: «Lord, what I need your grace to heal is...»

 

 

 

 

C. The Broken Mirror: Drawing and Restoration

Draw a large broken mirror with 6-8 fragments. On each fragment, write something that still reflects God's image in you despite the wound of sin: capacity to love, to create, to forgive, to show mercy, to seek truth, to offer friendship, to delight in beauty... Then, on the cracks between fragments, write what you need Christ to heal. This can be private; you don't need to show anyone. Finally, draw a second, restored mirror — or imagine what it looks like. What does the image of God look like when fully healed? (Hint: look at Mary, the Immaculate Conception.)

 

 

 

D. The Long Defeat and the Eucatastrophe: A Timeline

Draw a horizontal timeline of your life. Mark the long defeats — moments where sin, brokenness, or loss seemed to win. Use dark colors. Then, with a bright color, mark the eucatastrophes — the moments grace surprised you, when something impossible happened, when healing broke through where you least expected it. Finally, draw a cross at the center of the timeline. The eucatastrophe of the Cross stands over every long defeat. Even the defeats you haven't yet seen healed are under that Cross.

 

 

E. Look at Michelangelo's The Fall and Expulsion from Eden

Find an image online or ask your catechist to project it. Look at the contrast between the left side (the Temptation) and the right side (the Expulsion). Notice how the figures change: beauty becomes horror; intimacy becomes terror. Now look at an image of Our Lady of Guadalupe. Compare the two. The woman who helped bring sin into the world (Eve) is answered by the Woman who brought salvation into the world (Mary). The serpent who tempted in the garden is crushed under her feet. Where do you see yourself in these images?

 

 

F. The «Did God Really Say?» Journal

For one week, carry a small notebook. Every time you hear — from outside or from within — a voice that sounds like «Did God really say...?» write it down. It could be a doubt, a temptation, a rationalization, a cultural pressure. At the end of the week, look at your list. What patterns emerge? What is the serpent trying to get you to doubt? Then, next to each entry, write what God actually says — find a Scripture verse or a passage from the Catechism that answers the distortion. «I will put enmity between you and the woman»: learn to recognize the serpent's voice so you can resist it.

 

 

Memory Verse

«Where sin increased, grace abounded all the more, so that, just as sin exercised dominion in death, so grace might also exercise dominion through justification leading to eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord.»

— Romans 5:20-21

Prayer for This Week

Lord, you who created me in your image
and seek me even when I hide among the trees,
have mercy on my frailty.
I confess that I sometimes choose my own will
over your love,
that I listen to the serpent's whisper
when I should listen to your voice.
But I believe that your grace is greater than my sin,
that your mercy is wider than my wandering,
that where sin increased, grace abounded all the more.
May Mary, the new Eve, the woman who crushes the serpent,
teach me to say «yes» where I have said «no,»
to trust where I have doubted,
to stop hiding and let myself be found.
For you have been calling my name since Eden.
Here I am, Lord. I am here.
Amen.

Print Workbook
